1: Elijah’s drought announcement opens with the formula: ḥay YHWH ʾelōhê yiśrāʾēl ʾăšer ʿāmadtî lᵉpānāyw — ‘As YHWH the God of Israel lives, before whom I stand (lᵉpānāyw — to/before his face).’ The phrase ʿāmad lᵉpānāyw (stand before him) is the formula of prophetic investiture: the one who stands before YHWH has access to the divine council (sôḏ YHWH) and delivers its decrees as the divine messenger. Jer 23:18 makes this the mark of true prophecy: ‘for who among them has stood in the council of YHWH (baʿăṣat YHWH) to see and to hear his word?’ The false prophet speaks without standing; the true prophet speaks from standing before YHWH. The same formula describes the seraphim (‘standing above him,’ Isa 6:2), the angelic court in Job 1:6, and the Levitical ministry (‘to stand before YHWH to serve him,’ Deut 10:8). Heb 7:25 applies the standing-before-God formula to Christ’s perpetual intercession: ‘he always lives to make intercession for them’ — his permanent lᵉpānāyw presence before the Father is the fulfillment of what the prophet’s standing before YHWH anticipated, now without intermission and without the distance of messenger-relationship.
21: Elijah’s prayer over the widow’s dead son uses an unusual verb: wayyitmōdad ʿal hayyelεḏ šālōš pᵉʿāmîm — ‘he stretched/measured himself upon the child three times.’ The verb mādad (to measure) in the hitpael (reflexive/intensive) means ‘to stretch oneself out measuring against’ — a physical alignment of the prophet’s living body against the child’s dead body, repeated three times. The three-fold repetition (šālōš pᵉʿāmîm) marks intensity of intercessory prayer rather than mechanical technique: the life that returns comes from Elijah’s prayer (v21b: wayyiqrāʾ ʾel YHWH, ‘he cried to YHWH’), not from the physical posture alone. The Elisha parallel (2 Kgs 4:34: wayyiggahar ʿālaw, ‘he stretched himself over him’) establishes this as a prophetic pattern: physical closeness of the life-bearing prophet to the dead child as the vehicle of divine life-transfer. Paul over Eutychus (Acts 20:10: epipesōn autō, fell upon him) continues the pattern in apostolic form. The underlying theological claim in all three instances is that life-giving is a divine prerogative mediated through the prophet/apostle’s bodily and intercessory presence — a pattern whose ultimate ground is the incarnation: the Life of the world (John 11:25) taking on a body in order to give life through physical presence and sacrificial death.
2: wayᵉhî dᵉḇar-YHWH ʾēlāyw lēʾmōr — the word of YHWH came to him, saying. The divine-word reception formula occurs three times in ch.17 (vv.2, 8, 14/the oracle form), structuring the chapter as three successive divine directions. The brevity of v.2 — no elaboration, just the formula — intensifies the sense of immediate divine command after Elijah's drought announcement.
3: leḵ-lᵉḵā miṭṭāh wᵉp̄ānîṯā lᵉḵā qēḏmāh wᵉnistaraṯṯā bᵉnaḥal kᵉrîṯ — go from here, turn east, and hide yourself by the brook Cherith. Nistaraṯṭā (hithpael of sātar, to hide/conceal) — the command to hiddenness is theologically significant: Elijah disappears from Ahab's reach at YHWH's direction, not from fear. Cherith (kᵉrîṯ) east of the Jordan remains unidentified; the Jordan crossing removes Elijah from Ahab's jurisdiction entirely.
4: wᵉhāyāh min-hannaḥal tišṯeh wᵉʾeṯ-hāʿōrᵉḇîm ṣiwwîṯî lᵉkalkēlkāh šām — you will drink from the brook, and I have commanded (ṣiwwîṯî, perfect — already commanded) the ravens (hāʿōrᵉḇîm, H6158) to feed you there. The ravens were ritually unclean birds (Lev 11:15) — their use as divine provision agents subverts purity categories to serve prophetic sustenance. The perfect tense of the divine command (ṣiwwîṯî) treats the instruction as already accomplished before Elijah moves.
5: wayyēleḵ wayyaʿaś kidbar YHWH — he went and did according to the word of YHWH. The obedience report uses the exact formula of Abrahamic compliance (Gen 22:3 uses the same pattern). Wayyēšeḇ bᵉnaḥal kᵉrîṯ — he settled (yāšaḇ) by the brook Cherith — not a temporary stop but an established dwelling, underscoring the months-long duration of the provision.
6: wᵉhāʿōrᵉḇîm mᵉḇîʾîm lô leḥem ûḇāśār babbōqer wᵉleḥem ûḇāśār bāʿereḇ — the ravens brought him bread and meat in the morning and bread and meat in the evening. The double provision — morning and evening — mirrors the manna pattern (Exod 16:8, 12–13) and the Passover timing, connecting Elijah's sustenance to the wilderness provision of the Mosaic period. The source of the ravens' bread and meat is unspecified — the text focuses on the act of provision, not its logistics.
7: wayyᵉhî miqṣēh yāmîm wayyiḇaš hannaḥal kî lōʾ-hāyāh gešem bāʾāreṣ — after some time the brook dried up because there was no rain in the land. The phrase miqṣēh yāmîm (after the end of days) is deliberately vague — the drought was progressive, not instantaneous. The drying of Cherith forces the next divine direction; YHWH's provision does not insulate Elijah from the drought's effects but redirects him through them.
8: wayyᵉhî dᵉḇar-YHWH ʾēlāyw lēʾmōr — the second occurrence of the divine-word formula in ch.17 (cf. v.2). Its repetition at the moment of crisis (the dried brook) signals that YHWH's direction is responsive to changed circumstances, not static. The prophet is not left without instruction when his provision source fails.
9: qûm leḵ ṣārᵉpāṯāh ʾăšer lᵉṣîḏôn wᵉyāšaḇtā šām — go to Zarephath which belongs to Sidon and live there. Zarephath (ṣārᵉpaṯ, H6886 — modern Sarafand in Lebanon) was in Phoenician territory, the homeland of Jezebel's father Ethbaal. The theological irony: YHWH sends his prophet to the pagan widow in the land of Baal worship, not to any of Israel's many widows (Luke 4:25–26 makes this explicit). Ḥinnēh ṣiwwîṯî šām ʾišāh ʾalmānāh lᵉḵalkᵉlāk — I have commanded a widow there to feed you. The divine ṣiwwîṯî perfect reappears: the provision is already arranged before Elijah arrives.
10: wayyāqom wayyēleḵ ṣārᵉpāṯāh — Elijah rose and went to Zarephath. The obedience formula echoes v.5. At the city gate (pēṯaḥ hāʿîr) he finds the widow gathering sticks (mᵉqoššeš ʿēṣîm). Wayyiqrāʾ ʾēleyhā wayyōʾmar qᵉḥî-nāʾ lî mᵉʿaṭ-mayim bakkᵉlî wᵉʾešṯeh — he called to her and asked for a little water in a vessel to drink. The request begins with the least — water — before escalating to food.
11: wattēleḵ lāqaḥat wayyiqrāʾ ʾēleyhā wayyōʾmar qᵉḥî-nāʾ lî paṯ-leḥem bᵉyāḏēḵ — as she went to get it, he called again and asked for a piece of bread as well. The escalation of request tests the widow's hospitality. Her immediate compliance with the water request but the pause before bread (she must explain her situation in v.12) reveals the text's narrative pacing: the water was easy to give; the bread will require a miracle.
12: ḥay YHWH ʾĕlōhayiḵ ʾim-yeš-lî māṣôg — as YHWH your God lives, I have nothing baked. The widow's oath formula invokes YHWH ʾĕlōhayiḵ — YHWH your God (not 'my God' — she is Phoenician, outside the covenant). She swears by the prophet's God, acknowledging his deity's existence without personal covenant membership. Her situation: a handful of flour in a jar (kap-qemeḥ bakkad) and a little oil in a jug (mᵉʿaṭ-šemen baṣṣappāḥaṯ) — the minimum for one final meal before death by starvation.
13: wayyōʾmer ʾēleyhā ʾēlîyāhû ʾal-tîrʾî — Elijah said to her, do not be afraid. The prophetic reassurance formula (ʾal-tîrʾî) before the miracle command recurs throughout the prophetic narratives (cf. 2 Kgs 4:8–37). ʿăśî ḵaddāḇār ʾaḵ ʿăśî-lî mišṣām ʾūgāh qᵉṭannāh bāriʾšōnāh — but first make me a small cake from it and bring it out. The 'first-for-me' command requires priority hospitality to the prophet, testing the depth of her compliance with the divine commission to feed him (v.9).
14: kî-ḵōh ʾāmar YHWH ʾĕlōhê yiśrāʾēl kad-qemeḥ lōʾ tiḵleh wᵉṣappāḥaṯ-haššemen lōʾ tiḥsar — for thus says YHWH the God of Israel: the jar of flour will not be used up (lōʾ tiḵleh, will not be exhausted) and the jug of oil will not run dry (lōʾ tiḥsar, will not lack/fail). The divine oracle guarantees the miracle in advance of compliance. The two negatives with lōʾ + imperfect express permanent ongoing supply, not a one-time refill. ʿAḏ yôm tēṯ-YHWH gešem ʿal-pᵉnê hāʾădāmāh — until the day YHWH sends rain on the face of the earth — the miracle is temporally bounded by the drought oracle, connecting Zarephath provision to the Carmel confrontation to come.
15: wattēleḵ wattaʿaś kidbar ʾēlîyāhû — she went and did according to Elijah's word. The obedience formula applies now to the widow, paralleling the formula used for Elijah (v.5). Wattōʾkal hîʾ wᵉhûʾ ûḇêṯāh yāmîm — she and Elijah and her household ate for many days. The tripartite beneficiaries (widow, prophet, household) and the open-ended duration (yāmîm, days — many days) indicate the miracle was sustained provision, not a single instance.
16: kad-qemeḥ lōʾ-ḵālāṯāh wᵉṣappāḥaṯ-haššemen lōʾ-ḥāsēr kidbar YHWH ʾăšer dibber bᵉyad ʾēlîyāhû — the jar of flour was not exhausted and the jug of oil did not fail, according to the word of YHWH that he had spoken through Elijah. The fulfillment report mirrors the oracle of v.14 with verbal precision — the same nouns (kad-qemeḥ, ṣappāḥaṯ-haššemen), the same negated verbs (lōʾ ḵālāṯāh, lōʾ ḥāsēr). The fulfillment formula (kidbar YHWH ʾăšer dibber bᵉyad) marks the miracle as prophetic-word confirmation.
17: wayyᵉhî ʾaḥar haddᵉḇārîm hāʾēlleh ḥālāh ben-hāʾišāh baʿălaṯ habbāyiṯ — after these things the son of the woman who owned the house became ill. Wayyᵉhî ḥolyô ḥāzāq mᵉʾōḏ ʿaḏ ʾăšer lōʾ-nôṯᵃrāh bô nᵉšāmāh — his illness was so severe that no breath (nᵉšāmāh, H5397) remained in him. The term nᵉšāmāh (breath of life, the animating principle from Gen 2:7) is used here for its cessation — the child is clinically dead, not merely ill. The narrative escalation from provision miracle to resurrection sets up the climactic encounter.
18: wattōʾmer ʾel-ʾēlîyāhû mah-lî wālaḵ ʾîš hāʾĕlōhîm — she said to Elijah, what is between me and you, O man of God? The formula mah-lî wālaḵ (what to me and to you?) is an idiom of social distancing, expressing that the relationship has produced an unwanted outcome. Hābāʾṯā ʾēlay lᵉhazḵîr ʾeṯ-ʿăwōnî ûlᵉhamîṯ ʾeṯ-bᵉnî — have you come to call my sin to remembrance and to kill my son? She interprets the death as divine punishment triggered by the prophet's presence — a recognition that proximity to a holy man makes sin visible before God. The theology of divine holiness exposing human sin through prophetic presence is consistent with the Deuteronomistic framework.
19: wayyōʾmer ʾēleyhā tᵉnî-lî ʾeṯ-bᵉnēḵ wayyiqqāḥēhû mibᵉḥêqāh — give me your son. He took him from her arms (mibᵉḥêqāh, from her bosom/arms — the lap-embrace of nursing or mourning). Wayyaʿălēhû ʾel-hāʿălîyyāh ʾăšer-hûʾ yōšēḇ šām wayyaśkîḇēhû ʾal-miṭṭāṯô — he carried him up to the upper room where he was staying and laid him on his own bed. The upper room (ʿălîyyāh, H5944) is the private chamber of the guest; laying the child on the prophet's own bed enacts a transfer of prophetic life-force or intimate intercession.
20: wayyiqrāʾ ʾel-YHWH wayyōʾmar YHWH ʾĕlōhay hăgam ʿal-hāʾalmānāh ʾăšer-ʾănî miṯgôrēr ʿimmāh hārēʿōṯā lᵉhamîṯ ʾeṯ-bᵉnāh — he cried to YHWH: YHWH my God, have you also brought evil upon the widow with whom I am staying, to kill her son? Miṯgôrēr (hithpael of gûr, to sojourn) — Elijah presents himself as a dependent guest; the death of his host's son would bring reproach on his prophetic mission. The prayer is a bold challenge to divine action, consistent with the prophetic tradition of complaint-petition (cf. Moses, Habakkuk).
22: wayyišmaʿ YHWH bᵉqôl ʾēlîyāhû wattāšāḇ nefesh-hayyeled ʿal-qirḇô wayyeḥî — YHWH heard Elijah's voice, and the soul of the child (nefesh-hayyeled) returned to his inward parts (ʿal-qirḇô), and he revived (wayyeḥî). The term nefesh (H5315) returns to the body (qereb, H7130, the inner organs/interior) — the resurrection is anatomically described as the animating principle re-inhabiting the physical body. This is the first recorded resurrection in Scripture; the language of nefesh-return will recur in 2 Kgs 4:34–35 (Elisha) and Ezek 37 (valley of dry bones).
23: wayyiqqaḥ ʾēlîyāhû ʾeṯ-hayyeled wayyōrîḏēhû min-hāʿălîyyāh habbayᵉṯāh wayyittᵉnēhû lᵉʾimmô — Elijah took the child and brought him down from the upper room into the house and gave him to his mother. The descent from the upper room mirrors the ascent of v.19 — the spatial movement (up for death/prayer, down for restoration) enacts the reversal. Wayyōʾmer ʾēlîyāhû rᵉʾî ḥay bᵉnēḵ — Elijah said, see, your son lives. The announcement is direct and without elaboration: ḥay (alive), confirmed by presentation.
24: wattōʾmer hāʾišāh ʾel-ʾēlîyāhû ʿattāh zeh yāḏaʿtî kî ʾîš ʾĕlōhîm ʾattāh — the woman said, now I know that you are a man of God (ʾîš ʾĕlōhîm). The confession of the Sidonian widow mirrors the structural logic of the prophetic sign: the miracle authenticates the messenger. Ûḏᵉḇar-YHWH bᵉpîḵā ʾĕmeṯ — and the word of YHWH in your mouth is truth (ʾĕmeṯ, H571, faithfulness/reliability). The widow's conclusion is twofold: the prophet is genuine and YHWH's word is ʾĕmeṯ — the latter term carries the sense of durable reliability, not just factual accuracy. A Phoenician woman outside the covenant becomes a witness to YHWH's prophetic truthfulness.
